costs: Coordination overhead across multiple municipalities and tankerület boundaries
Transport costs for rotating specialist teachers between cluster sites
Building conversion/renovation costs for hub multi-service functions
content.equity_impact: Positive if well-designed: retains local access reducing commute burden and preserves community hub functions, particularly relevant given up to 94% disadvantage rates in some rural settlements; risk that voluntary networking is only adopted by better-organized villages, reproducing service inequality (F-live-portuguese_reform-03, F-live-portuguese_reform-10).
expected_benefits: Avoids school closure and the documented depopulation-acceleration risk associated with it (F-live-international_comparison-01, F-live-demography-07)
Addresses kindergarten and primary access gaps jointly through hub models
Provides specialist-teacher access via network rotation without full consolidation
Draws on evidenced multigrade-teaching quality practices
content.goal: Keep village primary schools open by pooling resources across multiple villages through inter-municipal pedagogical networks and multi-service hub models, avoiding closure while addressing the diseconomies of very small isolated schools.

implementation_steps.action: Define legal category for multi-village school networks with shared staffing allocations distinct from single-school funding formulas
Identify viable village clusters (2-5 settlements) based on travel distance and enrollment complementarity and pilot networked staffing in 5-10 clusters
Co-design multi-service hub conversion of school buildings, integrating kindergarten, library, or social services where enrollment alone cannot sustain a standalone school
Develop cross-school professional learning community and multigrade-teaching training modules for network-assigned teachers

mechanisms: French conventions ruralité demonstrate a reciprocal three-year State-municipality contract model where the State maintains teaching posts while municipalities reorganize school networks, with co-financing grants (F-live-international_comparison-03).
Poland's 2025 draft legislation proposes repurposing small-school infrastructure as social/cultural centers rather than binary closure, a template for hub conversion (F-live-polish_reform-06).

Kindergarten coverage gaps mirror primary-school gaps in the same villages and are governed by similar enrollment thresholds, so combining kindergarten and primary provision in a hub addresses both problems jointly (F-live-hungarian_education_system-08).

Historically, Hungary limited within-school segregation by avoiding closures via reduced parallel classes; networked retention could preserve this benefit while addressing enrollment viability (F-live-education_finance-09, F-live-hungarian_education_system-05).

Multigrade/mixed-age teaching models show quality potential when supported by deliberate teacher training and cross-school professional networks, as demonstrated in Chile and Norway, informing the pedagogical design of clusters (F-live-portuguese_reform-07).
risks: Success may be contingent on rare local social capital and bridging networks, as in the single documented Erdőkürt case, limiting scalability (F-live-portuguese_reform-10)
Inter-municipal competition for pupils (documented informal competition dynamics) could undermine cooperative network design (F-live-school_network_planning-09)
No dedicated statutory closure/network procedure currently exists, creating implementation ambiguity absent new legislation (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)
content.title: Networked retention: multi-village pedagogical clusters and multi-service community hubs
